Global Markets Fall as Tech Bubble Jitters Resurface
data.description:
By Dow Jones Newswires Staff


Stock markets in Asia and Europe followed Wall Street lower after Thursday's rally evaporated. AI-chip maker Nvidia gave up early gains to close sharply lower in the prior session, dragging down the main indexes as fears of an artificial-intelligence bubble resurfaced. Asian and European semiconductor stocks tumbled Friday. Still, U.S. stock futures pointed to a slightly higher open.

Meanwhile, the U.S. dollar fell in the wake of the delayed September nonfarm payrolls report Thursday and bitcoin continued its slump.


--U.S. futures for the S&P 500 were up 0.2%, futures for the Dow Jones Industrial Average climbed 0.3%, while futures tied to the Nasdaq edged up around 0.1%. Changes in futures do not necessarily predict movements after the opening bell.

--South Korea's Kospi Composite ended down 3.8% as shares in Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix--the world's two largest memory-chip makers--slumped. Shares in SK Hynix tumbled 8.8% by the close. Taiwan's TAIEX fell 3.6%. "The reversal in Nvidia and the broader U.S. tech space has forced investors to reassess whether AI spending can deliver near-term earnings, which directly hit markets like Korea and Taiwan where semiconductors dominate benchmark weights," Tareck Horchani, head of prime brokerage dealing at Maybank Securities, said.

--Hong Kong's Hang Seng declined 2.4%, while Japan's Nikkei 225 index fell 2.4%. Japan's cabinet under new Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi on Friday approved $135 billion of stimulus, expectations for which have seen Japanese government bond yields rise on concerns over the country's fiscal position. Meanwhile, Japanese Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama expressed concern about the rapidly weakening yen.

--Shares in European semiconductor companies also fell. Among national indexes, Amsterdam's tech heavy AEX was the biggest decliner--down 1.2%--lead by ASML, ASM and BE Semiconductor, which were all down over 4% in early trade. German chip maker Infineon Technologies fell 3.8% and STMicroelectronics, which supplies Apple and Tesla, was down 2%. The pan-European Stoxx Europe 600 was recently down 1%.

--Bitcoin fell to a seven-month low after the declines in U.S. stocks overnight. "Adding to the negative backdrop for crypto were lingering questions over the crypto market structure bill that's being worked on in Congress," Deutsche Bank analysts said in a note. With bitcoin now more than 30% below its peak, there are worries about further forced selling as retail investors might need to liquidate other assets to meet margin calls, they said. Bitcoin was recently down 2.5% to a low of $82,032, according to LSEG data.

--The U.S. dollar eased with market-watchers uncertain about the Federal Reserve's rate path. "We still expect the Fed to stay on hold in December, with markets currently pricing around 32% probability of a cut," Danske Bank analysts said in a note. The DXY dollar index against a basket of major currencies was down 0.1% to 100.085.

--U.S. Treasury yields declined, extending Thursday's moves in the wake of mixed signals from the delayed September jobs report. The two- and 10-year Treasury yields were down 1 basis point at 3.546% and 4.093%, respectively, according to Tradeweb.

--Yields on U.K. government bonds eased after weaker-than-expected U.K. retail sales data for October. The 10-year gilt yield fell 2 basis points to last trade at 4.561%.

--In commodities, oil prices extended their losses after Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky agreed to work on a U.S.-drafted peace plan, easing geopolitical concerns. Brent crude fell 1.9% to 57.89 a barrel, while WTI was down 1.6% to $62.36 a barrel.


Write to Barcelona editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-21-25 0432ET

data.title: Italy Awaits Possible Moody's Upgrade After Years of Stagnation
data.description:
(Alliance News) - Italy's current rating with Moody's stands at Baa3, the lowest tier of the lower medium grade, a level it shares with Romania, Greece, Colombia, and India.

As highlighted by MF-Milano Finanza, on May 23 the agency raised Italy's outlook from stable to positive, opening the door to a potential upgrade that would mark the first since 2002, when the rating was lifted to Aa2 from Aa3. Following years of downgrades culminating in the Baa3 rating in 2018--unchanged since--analysts are now expressing cautious optimism.

Gianni Piazzoli, CIO of Vontobel, points to two factors that could support an upgrade: an improvement in macroeconomic indicators since May and a favorable gap between the Italian government's forecasts and those of Moody's.

The deficit is expected to reach 3% in 2025, down from 3.4% in 2024, and to fall further to 2.8% in 2026. The debt-to-GDP ratio should settle at 137.5% in 2026, compared to Moody's estimate of 138.4%. Italy also recorded a primary surplus in 2024, projected to grow from 0.5% of GDP last year to 2% by 2028.

Vontobel emphasizes the contrast with France, which, despite enjoying a Moody's rating six notches higher, has posted a negative primary surplus for several years and is expected to reach a debt-to-GDP ratio of 120% by 2027. However, Italy's possible exit from the EU's Excessive Deficit Procedure will depend on the 2025 deficit data due in April. Citi analysts note that Italy's public finances are "positively surprising," thanks in part to the end of the superbonus program and employment gains tied to the National Recovery and Resilience Plan (Pnrr).

According to UniCredit, a potential upgrade would be "another step in the constructive trend regarding creditworthiness." Other positive signals include the narrowing of the BTP/Bund spread to 75 basis points, while the French spread has risen to 77.

On October 24, Moody's confirmed France's Aa3 rating but lowered its outlook to negative. Filippo Mormando of BBVA suggests that the agency's past strictness towards Italy and its current more lenient approach to other countries could signal an improvement "within 12-18 months," potentially by up to two notches, to realign the rating with those of other agencies.

By Antonio Di Giorgio, Alliance News reporter

Comments and questions to redazione@alliancenews.com

Copyright 2025 Alliance News IS Italian Service Ltd. All rights reserved.

data.title: Snam and Adia Cancel Acquisition of Stake in Oge, Says German Economy Ministry
data.description:
The decision to cancel the planned purchase of a stake in Open Grid Europe (Oge), the German gas transmission network, was made by the potential buyers--Italy's Snam and indirect shareholder State Grid--as well as the prospective seller, Adia.

This was announced by the German Economy Ministry.

In April, Snam had agreed to acquire a 24.99% stake in Vier Gas Holding, the owner of Oge, from an Adia division based in Abu Dhabi. The deal was called off a week ago following a prolonged review by Berlin.

Sources told Reuters that Germany had concerns about the involvement of China, as State Grid--the indirect shareholder of Snam--is Chinese-owned, in what could have become a transaction involving a key part of the country's critical energy infrastructure.

"Foreign trade regulations allow for the review of investments by non-EU entities," the German Economy Ministry stated.

"Accordingly, in this case, the review focused exclusively on the planned indirect investment by the Chinese state-owned group State Grid Corporation of China, which holds an indirect stake in the Italian company Snam," the ministry added.

(Translated by Claudio Leonel Piacquadio, editing by Stefano Bernabei)

data.title: Government Establishes Food Price Commission to Ensure Impact of Reduced Food VAT
data.description:
The government is establishing a Food Price Commission to ensure that the temporary reduction of food VAT from 12 to 6 percent in 2026 will genuinely result in lower prices at the checkout.

The Swedish Consumer Agency and the National Institute of Economic Research have been tasked with monitoring and analyzing food prices, assessing whether the VAT reduction is fully reflected in consumer prices, and clarifying which other factors are influencing price developments. This move comes after several years of strained household finances and a previous inflation rate of ten percent.

Not All Tariffs Costs Have Passed Through: JPM's Feroli
data.description:
Michael Feroli, chief US economist at JPMorgan Securities, says a December rate cut will be a "very close call." He tells Romaine Bostick and Katie Greifeld on "The Close" that tariff-related price pressures likely haven't fully passed through and core inflation may stay in the upper-2% range longer than the Fed would prefer.

Bloomberg videos, provided by MT Newswires

EQT Acquires Berlin Property Portfolio with 477 Apartments
data.description:
Private equity firm EQT, through EQT Real Estate, has acquired a property portfolio comprising 17 buildings with a total of 477 apartments in Mahlow, located on the southern outskirts of Berlin, Germany.

The acquisition also includes six commercial units and 520 parking spaces. This information was disclosed in a press release, which did not provide any financial details regarding the transaction.

data.title: EUROPE POWER-Surging wind power output to pressure prompt prices
Tech stocks still set for record $75 billion inflow in 2025, says BofA
data.description:
LONDON (Reuters) -Tech stocks remain on track for a record $75 billion inflow this year, BofA's weekly flow show note said on Friday, highlighting demand for the sector even as it comes under pressure from concerns about lofty valuations.

Tech stocks have been on a surge for much of this year, and the Nasdaq has risen around 14% and hit an all time high in late October.

But they have since stumbled and the tech heavy index dropped 2% on Thursday.

Nonetheless, tech stocks also saw a $4.4 billion inflow in the week to Wednesday, BofA said in their weekly round up of flows in and out of world markets.

Elsewhere, it said crypto funds saw a weekly outflow of $2.2 billion, the second largest outflow on record.

Cryptocurrencies have been caught in a broad flight from risk assets and on Friday bitcoin and ether fell to multi-month lows.

The BofA note also showed U.S. Treasuries had $8.8 billion of inflows, their biggest inflow since April when U.S. President Donald Trump's Liberation Day tariffs roiled world markets.

(Reporting by Dhara Ranasignhe, editing by Alun John)

data.title: Carlsberg Boosts Share of Renewable Electricity in the Nordics with New PPA Agreements
data.description:
The brewing giant Carlsberg has signed three long-term power purchase agreements (PPAs) that will increase its supply of renewable electricity in Norway, Sweden, and Finland.

Ringnes, Carlsberg's Norwegian subsidiary, will purchase 435 gigawatt hours from the Fennefoss hydropower plant over a ten-year period. Carlsberg Sweden will source electricity from the Orken wind farm in Halland, while Sinebrychoff, the Finnish unit, will buy volumes from a wind farm in Paltusmaki operated by Encavis.

These agreements will raise the group's total coverage from PPAs from around 10 percent to 21 percent, as the company accelerates its transition in the Nordic region.
